返回本批次

Nanocode：200 美元可以在 TPU 上的纯 JAX 中买到的最好的 Claude 代码

Nanocode: The best Claude Code that $200 can buy in pure JAX on TPUs

github.com 作者 desideratum 分数 184 评论 24 2026/04/05 22:21
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 我很高兴分享纳米代码。这是一个向您展示如何端到端训练您自己的克劳德代码的库。首先，我们将遵循使用宪法人工智能进行训练的最简单的方法——Anthropic 用来训练他们的 Claude 模型的方法。我们将编写自己的 SOUL.md，定义我们的模型将用来与世界交互的代理接口，生成合成数据，并使用偏好优化来使模型与我们的 SOUL 保持一致。 nanocode 完全用 JAX 编写，旨在使用 TPU 进行训练。我改编了 Karpathy 令人难以置信的 nanochat 项目的核心培训基础设施和理念，因此，如果您熟悉 nanochat，那么 nanocode 应该感觉非常相似。这就是我的 d24 1.3B 参数纳码的结果：
  2. 纳码.mp4
  3. 您可以免费开始使用 Google TRC 计划，该计划让您可以免费使用一个月的抢占式 TPU - 我认为新的 Google Cloud 帐户还可以获得 300 美元的积分。我很幸运能够在这个项目中使用 TRC 计划 3 个月，而且我发现大多数时候我的 Spot 实例很少被中断，我可以轻松地将同一个 Pod 运行一周或更长时间。您可以在花费 200 美元的 TPU v6e-8 上总共大约 9 小时内重现 nanocode-d24（1.3B 参数），或者在花费 34 美元的大约 1.5 小时内训练 nanocode-d20（477M 参数）。如果您使用的是 NVIDIA GPU，纳码也应该可以开箱即用，但您应该知道纳码已经针对 TPU 进行了高度优化。训练 nanocode：友好的代理编码合作伙伴 Andrej 的 nanochat 原始发布帖子很好地解释了我们在这里所做的事情，并且您将在 nanocode 中使用的命令几乎相同，因此我建议首先阅读他的工作。我将回顾一下我们为从模型中引出代理编码行为而采取的不同做法。分词和预训练预训练和分词器训练过程与 nanochat 几乎相同，但我发现在预训练和分词器混合中以 1:5 的比例包含来自 Stack-V2 的额外编码数据会产生更强大的编码模型和更高效的代码分词，这有很大帮助。首先，我们下载分词器训练和模型预训练所需的数据集分片： # 我们将训练 d24、1.3B 参数模型。但您可以根据您的模型尺寸调整 MODEL_TAG。 export NANOCODE_BASE_DIR="$HOME/.cache/nanocode" export MODEL_TAG=d24 python -m data.pretrain -dfineweb-edu -n 300 # 我已经预先打包并分片了类似于 FinewWeb 的 Stack python -m data.pretrain -d the-stack-v2-dedup -n 60 并启动我们的分词器训练脚本： python -m script.tok_train --max-chars=2000000000 python -m script.tok_eval 作为参考，我们可以与 nanochat 的 tokenizer 进行比较，除了在训练混合物中添加 The Stack 之外，它是相同的（好吧，我还添加了特殊的 token 和模板逻辑来支持更复杂的工具调用，但稍后会详细介绍）。比较：nanocode vs nanochat ======================================================================================================= 文本类型字节 nanocode nanochat 相对更好的令牌比率 令牌比率差异 % -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 新闻 1819 407 4.47 375 4.85 +7.9% nanochat 韩语 893 558 1.60 712 1.25 -27.6% nanocode 代码 1259 326 3.86 492 2.56 -50.9% nanocode 数学 1834 922 1.99 966 1.90 -4.8% nanocode 科学 1112 259 4.29 228 4.88 +12.0% nanochat fwe-train 4208518 902950 4.66 856883 4.91 +5.1% nanochat fwe-val 4495276 975403 4.61 1010352 4.86 -3.6% nanocode 我们可以看到，这给出了代码的巨大提升是以一般文本标记化效率为代价的，但这没关系，因为我们希望我们的模型能够很好地完成一件事；代理编码。我们的模型使用 8 的参数：数据比进行训练（遵循 nanochat 的缩放定律分析）。让我们像这样开始训练： python -u -m script.base_train \ --batch-size=32 \ --minibatch-size=1 \ --config=configs.d24 \ --eval-every=500 \ --sample-every=500 您应该看到类似这样的内容： Vocab size: 32768 World size: 8 1342.17728M 模型参数67.108864M wte 参数 1207.959552M h 参数 67.108864M lm_head 参数 在 10737418240 个令牌上进行 10241 个步骤的训练 ==================== 每个令牌的估计 FLOPs：10066329600 缩放 AdamW 参数的 LR ∝1/√(2048/768) = 0.612372 步长：0/10241 |损失：10.398 |结束时间：104.58 秒 | | tkps: 10026 | MFU：1.37 |预计到达时间：-1.0 分钟 | lr_multiplier：1.000 峰值字节保留/限制：14.86/22.27 步长：1/10241 |损失：9.771 | dt: 2.74 秒 | | tkps: 382082 | MFU：52.37 |预计到达时间：-1.0 分钟 | lr_multiplier: 1.000 步长: 2/10241 |损失：8.209 | dt: 2.74 秒 | | tkps: 382220 | MFU：52.39 |预计到达时间：234.1 分钟 | lr_multiplier: 1.000 步骤: 3/10241 |损失：7.327 | dt: 2.74 秒 | | tkps: 382193 | MFU：52.39 |预计到达时间：312.1 分钟 | lr_multiplier: 1.000 ... fwe_bpb: 0.7626 | sv2_bpb：0.4356 |平均bpb：0.5991 | dt: 90.53s <|bos|>法国的首都是巴黎。它是法国最大的城市，也是<|bos|>人口最多的城市。黄金的化学符号是Au。黄金是一种柔软、有延展性的黄色金属，距离太阳最近的行星是水星，它是太阳系中最小的行星。它是最接近<|bos|>热的反义词是冷。冷的反义词是热。热的反义词是冷。 <|bos|>一周的倒数第二天是主日。 （利未记 23:2）...核心指标：0.2352 | dt：56.86s 总训练时间：467.15min
  4. 我们的模型已经获得了一些关于世界的知识，这很好。但它仍然不知道星期六:)。让我们看一些更彻底的定量结果，因为我们在训练期间只使用评估数据的较小子集来估计指标： python -u -m script.base_eval --checkpoint=base --minibatch-size=8 这将打印一大堆指标，但相关指标是我们预训练集中的每字节位数：sv2 (The Stack V2) 和 fwe (FineWeb_EDU)，以及与 nanochat 的结果进行比较的 CORE 指标GPT-2 简单明了。我已经编译了几个模型参数大小的结果，以了解我们的缩放定律：深度|参数 |核心|成本|时间 | MFU | fwe bpb | sv2 bpb | |--------|--------|------|------|--------|--------|---------|---------| | d12 | 135M | 0.090| 3 美元 | 9 分钟 | 17.4% | 0.956 | 0.956 0.689 | 0.689 | d20 | 477M | 0.170| 30 美元 | 1.4小时| 45.2% | 0.838 | 0.838 0.533 | 0.533 | d24 | d24 | 1.3B| 0.227| 200 美元 | 9.3 小时| 52.5% | 0.759 | 0.759 0.445 | 0.445由于 CORE 衡量一般语言推理能力，并且我们已将模型针对代码数据进行调整，因此预计我们的 CORE 分数与相应的 GPT-2 模型相比会略有下降。仅在 FineWeb-EDU 上训练 d24 的 CORE 分数为 0.261，与下面的 GPT-2 XL 和 nanochat-d24 一致。这里的权衡是我们希望我们的模型在编码任务中表现良好。 |型号|参数|核心| |----------------|--------|--------| | GPT-2 小号 | 124M | 0.114 | 0.114 | GPT-2 中型 | 355M| 0.185 | 0.185 | GPT-2 大号 | 774M | 0.215 | 0.215 | GPT-2 XL | 1.6B| 0.257 | 0.257在这篇文章中，我主要指的是我们的 d24 模型，它类似于 nanochat 的 d24 模型，但使用两倍的上下文长度（4096 与 2048）进行训练，以更好地支持多轮代理对话。现在我们已经有了一个相当强大的编码基础模型，让我们看看如何将其变成一个成熟的代理编码合作伙伴。代理马拉松 让我们从基本原理出发思考一下代理模型正在做什么。预训练 LLM 会产生下一个令牌生成器，这些生成器压缩了大量知识，但它们对于遵循指令、回答有关其所拥有知识的问题或修复 Python 文件中的错误等事情并没有真正的用处。为了让我们的模型做有用的事情，还有很多工作要做。第一步是模板化 - 界定输入和输出的不同组成部分，以便模型了解要求执行的任务的结构。让我们以聊天模板为例。对话可以按轮流进行组织，每一方一次轮流 - 因此我们的模型需要知道轮到谁，以及他们说了些什么。用户：2+2是什么？ Assistant: 4 我们可以将其模板化为： <|bos|><|user_start|>什么是 2+2?<|user_end|> <|assistant_start|>4<|assistant_end|>
  5. <|user_start|>、<|user_end|>、<|assistant_start|> 和 <|assistant_end|> 是特殊标记，有助于为原始文本提供结构。我们通常在代币化时为他们保留整个代币。伟大的。现在让我们考虑一下可以用于代理模型的模板类型。代理行为的基础是工具调用——一种模型的轮流不针对用户的任务，而是可以通过与现实世界的接口进行操作，并产生模型可以实时响应的输出。如果我们这样看，工具调用的输出可以被视为另一种轮次，因此我们保留两个额外的特殊标记<|tool_result_start|>和<|tool_result_end|>，这样我们的模型就知道信息何时来自工具调用，而不是用户。现在我们只需要一种方法让我们的模型知道如何进行工具调用 - 我们需要模型希望调用的工具名称的模板以及（可选）它需要传递的任何关键字参数。让我们以 grep 为例： # 在 src/ 目录中递归搜索术语“TODO” grep -rn "TODO" src/ # 这看起来像这样： <|assistant_start|> 我将搜索 TODO。 <|tool_call_start|>Bash<|tool_arg|>命令<|tool_val|>grep -rn "TODO" src/<|tool_call_end|> <|assistant_end|>

Original Summary

  1. I'm so excited to share nanocode. This is a library showing you how to train your own Claude Code end-to-end. To a first approximation, we will follow the simplest possible approach for training using Constitutional AI - the approach used by Anthropic to train their Claude models. We'll write our own SOUL.md, define the agentic interface which our model will use to interact with the world, generate synthetic data, and use preference optimisation to align the model with our SOUL. nanocode is written entirely in JAX and designed to be trained using TPUs. I adapted the core training infrastructure and philosophy from Karpathy's incredible nanochat project, so if you're familiar with nanochat, nanocode should feel very similar. This is how my d24 1.3B parameter nanocode turned out:
  2. nanocode.mp4
  3. You can get started for free using the Google TRC program which gives you free access to pre-emptible TPUs for a month - and I think new Google Cloud accounts also get $300 in credits. I was fortunate to have access to the TRC program for 3 months for this project, and I found most of the time that my spot instances were rarely interrupted and I could easily have the same pod up for a week or more. You can reproduce nanocode-d24 (1.3B params) in around ~9 hours in total on a TPU v6e-8 costing $200, or train nanocode-d20 (477M params) in ~1.5 hours costing $34. If you're using NVIDIA GPUs, nanocode should also work out of the box, but you should be aware that nanocode has been highly optimised for TPUs. Training nanocode: a friendly agentic coding partner Andrej's original release post for nanochat does a great job of explaining what we're doing here, and the commands you'll use in nanocode are virtually identical, so I'd recommend reading through his work first. I'll go over what we've done differently to elicit agentic coding behaviours from our model. Tokenization and Pre-training The pre-training and tokenizer training process is pretty much identical to nanochat's, but I found that including additional coding data from The Stack-V2 at a ratio of 1:5 in both the pre-training and tokenizer mixture resulted in a stronger coding model and more efficient code tokenization, which helped a ton. Let's first download the dataset shards we'll need for tokenizer training and model pre-training: # we'll be training our d24, 1.3B parameter model. but you can adapt MODEL_TAG for your model size. export NANOCODE_BASE_DIR="$HOME/.cache/nanocode" export MODEL_TAG=d24 python -m data.pretrain -d fineweb-edu -n 300 # I've pre-packed and sharded The Stack similar to FinewWeb python -m data.pretrain -d the-stack-v2-dedup -n 60 And kick off our tokenizer training script: python -m scripts.tok_train --max-chars=2000000000 python -m scripts.tok_eval For reference, we can compare with nanochat's tokenizer which is identical aside from the addition of The Stack in the training mixture (well, I've also added special tokens and templating logic to support more sophisticated tool calling, but more on that later). Comparison: nanocode vs nanochat =============================================================================================== Text Type Bytes nanocode nanochat Relative Better Tokens Ratio Tokens Ratio Diff % ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- news 1819 407 4.47 375 4.85 +7.9% nanochat korean 893 558 1.60 712 1.25 -27.6% nanocode code 1259 326 3.86 492 2.56 -50.9% nanocode math 1834 922 1.99 966 1.90 -4.8% nanocode science 1112 259 4.29 228 4.88 +12.0% nanochat fwe-train 4208518 902950 4.66 856883 4.91 +5.1% nanochat fwe-val 4495276 975403 4.61 1010352 4.86 -3.6% nanocode We can see that this gives a big boost for code at the cost of general text tokenization efficiency, but this is okay since we want our model to do one thing very well; agentic coding. Our models are trained with a param:data ratio of 8 (following nanochat's scaling law analysis). Let's kick off a training run like so: python -u -m scripts.base_train \ --batch-size=32 \ --minibatch-size=1 \ --config=configs.d24 \ --eval-every=500 \ --sample-every=500 You should see something like this: Vocab size: 32768 World size: 8 1342.17728M model parameters 67.108864M wte parameters 1207.959552M h parameters 67.108864M lm_head parameters Training on 10737418240 tokens over 10241 steps ==================== Estimated FLOPs per token: 10066329600 Scaling the LR for the AdamW parameters ∝1/√(2048/768) = 0.612372 Step: 0/10241 | Loss: 10.398 | dt: 104.58s | | tkps: 10026 | mfu: 1.37 | ETA: -1.0 min | lr_multiplier: 1.000 Peak bytes reserved/limit: 14.86/22.27 Step: 1/10241 | Loss: 9.771 | dt: 2.74s | | tkps: 382082 | mfu: 52.37 | ETA: -1.0 min | lr_multiplier: 1.000 Step: 2/10241 | Loss: 8.209 | dt: 2.74s | | tkps: 382220 | mfu: 52.39 | ETA: 234.1 min | lr_multiplier: 1.000 Step: 3/10241 | Loss: 7.327 | dt: 2.74s | | tkps: 382193 | mfu: 52.39 | ETA: 312.1 min | lr_multiplier: 1.000 ... fwe_bpb: 0.7626 | sv2_bpb: 0.4356 | avg_bpb: 0.5991 | dt: 90.53s <|bos|>The capital of France is Paris. It is the largest city in France and the most populous city in <|bos|>The chemical symbol of gold is Au. Gold is a soft, malleable, yellow metal that is <|bos|>The closest planet to the Sun is Mercury, which is the smallest planet in the solar system. It is the closest <|bos|>The opposite of hot is cold. The opposite of cold is heat. The opposite of heat is cold. <|bos|>The second-last day of the week is the day of the Lord. (Leviticus 23:2) ... CORE metric: 0.2352 | dt: 56.86s Total training time: 467.15min
  4. Our model has attained some knowledge about the world, which is nice. It still doesn't know about Saturday though : ). Let's look at some more thorough quantitative results, since we only estimate metrics using a smaller subset of the evaluation data during training: python -u -m scripts.base_eval --checkpoint=base --minibatch-size=8 This will print a whole bunch of metrics, but the relevant ones are bits-per-byte across our pretraining sets: sv2 (The Stack V2) and fwe (FineWeb_EDU), and the CORE metric which makes comparing against nanochat's results and GPT-2 straightforward. I've compiled the results across a few model parameter sizes to get a feel for our scaling laws: | depth | params | CORE | cost | time | MFU | fwe bpb | sv2 bpb | |-------|--------|------|------|--------|-------|---------|---------| | d12 | 135M | 0.090| $3 | 9 min | 17.4% | 0.956 | 0.689 | | d20 | 477M | 0.170| $30 | 1.4 hrs| 45.2% | 0.838 | 0.533 | | d24 | 1.3B | 0.227| $200 | 9.3 hrs| 52.5% | 0.759 | 0.445 | Since CORE measures general language reasoning capabilities and we've geared our models towards code data, it's expected that our CORE scores drop slightly compared to the corresponding GPT-2 models. Training d24 on FineWeb-EDU alone resulted in a CORE score of 0.261 which lines up with GPT-2 XL below and nanochat-d24. The tradeoff here is that we expect our models to perform well in coding tasks. | model | params | CORE | |---------------|--------|-------| | GPT-2 Small | 124M | 0.114 | | GPT-2 Medium | 355M | 0.185 | | GPT-2 Large | 774M | 0.215 | | GPT-2 XL | 1.6B | 0.257 | I'll mostly be referring to our d24 model throughout this post, which is similar to nanochat's d24 model but is trained with twice the context length (4096 vs. 2048) to better support multi-turn agentic conversations. Now that we have a reasonably capable coding base model, let's look at how we can turn it into a fully-fledged agentic coding partner. The Agentic Marathon Let's think a bit about what agentic models are doing from first principles. Pre-training LLMs produces next-token-generators which have compressed a vast amount of knowledge, but they aren't really useful for things like following instructions, answering questions about the knowledge they have, or fixing bugs in Python files. There's a bunch more work to do in trying to get our models to do useful things. The first step is templating - delimiting different components of the input and output so the model learns the structure of the task it's being asked to perform. Let's take chat templating as an example. Conversation can be structured as turns, where each side takes a turn at a time - so our model needs to know whose turn it is, and what they've said. User: What is 2+2? Assistant: 4 We can template this as: <|bos|><|user_start|>What is 2+2?<|user_end|> <|assistant_start|>4<|assistant_end|>
  5. <|user_start|>, <|user_end|>, <|assistant_start|>, and <|assistant_end|> are special tokens which help provide structure to raw text. We typically reserve a whole token for them when tokenizing. Great. Now let's think about the kind of templating we might use for an agentic model. The basis for agentic behaviour is tool-calling - a kind of task where the model's turn isn't directed towards the user, but may instead be an action through an interface with the real world, and which produces outputs which the model may respond to in real-time. If we look at it this way the outputs of a tool call can just be treated as another kind of turn, so we reserve two additional special tokens<|tool_result_start|> and <|tool_result_end|> so our model knows when information is coming from a tool call, and not the user. Now we just need a way to let our model know how to make tool calls - we'll need templating for the name of the tool the model wishes to invoke and (optionally) any keyword-arguments it needs to pass through. Let's take grep as an example: # search recursively for the term "TODO" in the src/ directory grep -rn "TODO" src/ # This would look something like this: <|assistant_start|> I'll search for TODO. <|tool_call_start|>Bash<|tool_arg|>command<|tool_val|>grep -rn "TODO" src/<|tool_call_end|> <|assistant_end|>

评论（默认中文）

wwfn 2026/04/06 01:11
切题（但主题是“威胁很舒服地转向不理解你在做什么”也在首页上）：

示例中生成的python代码是否错误？

提示

> 开发一个 Python 函数，从列表中删除任何错误值。返回修改后的列表而不创建新列表。

通过列表理解来回答，这会生成一个新列表并保持原始列表不变（不要介意 *args 输入不一定是可修改列表？）

   def remove_falsey_values(*args): return [val for val in args if val]
而我期望类似的东西

    def remove_falsey_values(l):
          for i in reversed(range(len(l))):
               if not l[i]: l.pop(i)
          # returned list is linked to input l 
          return l

    a = [1, 0, False, 'foo']
    x = remove_falsey_values(a)
    x[0] = 2
    print(a) # [2,'foo']
Tangential (but topical in that "The threat is comfortable drift toward not understanding what you're doing" is also on the front page):

Is the generated python code in the example wrong?

The prompt

> Develop a Python function that removes any falsey values from a list. Return the modified list without creating a new one.

Is answered with list comprehension, which makes a new list and leaves the original unmodified (never mind that the *args input necessarily can't be a modifiable list?)

   def remove_falsey_values(*args): return [val for val in args if val]
Whereas I'd expect something like

    def remove_falsey_values(l):
          for i in reversed(range(len(l))):
               if not l[i]: l.pop(i)
          # returned list is linked to input l 
          return l

    a = [1, 0, False, 'foo']
    x = remove_falsey_values(a)
    x[0] = 2
    print(a) # [2,'foo']
在 HN 中查看
hecanjog 2026/04/06 01:22
它不符合就地修改列表的要求，但提示本身明确要求实现使用 *args 和列表理解，从而与要求相矛盾。
It doesn't fit the requirement to modify the list in place, but the prompt itself contradicts the requirements by asking explicitly for the implementation to use *args and a list comprehension.
在 HN 中查看
wwfn 2026/04/06 01:33
啊，我没有看到完整的原始提示——它对我来说已经溢出到水平卷轴中了。我认为这是“批评循环”注入了 *args 要求。我猜是垃圾进来，垃圾出去。仍然是不幸的例子。
Ahh I didn't see the full original prompt -- it's overflowing into a horz scroll for me. I thought it was the "critique loop" that injected the *args requirement. I guess garbage in, garbage out. Still unfortunate example to use.
在 HN 中查看
desideratum 2026/04/06 04:17
哦，我不会感到惊讶。这是我使用的 OSS 代码数据集之一的示例，这些数据集都是使用 LLM 综合生成的。数据确实是护城河。
Oh I wouldn't be surprised. This is a sample from one of the OSS code datasets I'd used, which are all generated synthetically using LLMs. Data is indeed the moat.
在 HN 中查看
__s 2026/04/06 03:04 

    def remove_falsey_values(l):
          l[:] = (x for x in l if x)

    def remove_falsey_values(l):
          l[:] = (x for x in l if x)
在 HN 中查看
nusl 2026/04/06 06:22
为什么要修改原始列表并在第二个示例中返回它？老实说第一个更好
Why would you modify the original list and return it with the second example? Honestly the first is better
在 HN 中查看
highphive 2026/04/06 06:26
问题并不是更好的做法，问题是代码是否遵循提示。第一个例子没有。
The question isn't really what's better practice, the question is whether the code follows the prompt. The first example does not.

在 HN 中查看
semiinfinitely 2026/04/06 04:22
你的第二个函数是你从尝试像 c 那样对 python 进行编程的人那里得到的错误代码类型
your second function is the type of bad code you get from people trying to program python like its c
在 HN 中查看
ktm5j 2026/04/06 07:15
有没有一种Python式的方法来满足提示？ IE不用新建一个目录吗？
Is there a pythonic way to satisfy the prompt? IE without making a new list?
在 HN 中查看
wwfn 2026/04/06 07:03
绝对地！并且 list.pop 版本慢了多个数量级。但我按照提示要求对现有列表进行就地修改。理解力并不能做到这一点。
Absolutely! And the list.pop version is multiple orders of magnitude slower. But I took the prompt to be asking for in-place modification of the existing list. Comprehension does not do that.
在 HN 中查看
bdbdbdb 2026/04/06 00:21
愚蠢的问题 - 我并不是想削弱这里的成就，我只是真的不明白：

既然有免费的编码模型，为什么人们愿意花 200 美元来训练编码模型呢？

Dumb question - and I'm not trying diminish the achievement here, I just genuinely don't understand:

Why would people want to spend $200 to train a coding model when there are free coding models?

在 HN 中查看
desideratum 2026/04/06 00:30
这是一个很好的问题。你绝对不是在训练它使用它，而是在训练它理解事物是如何工作的。这是一个教育项目，如果您有兴趣尝试 JAX 中的分布式训练技术或偏好优化等技术，这将为您提供一个最小且可破解的库来构建。
This is a great question. You definitely aren't training this to use it, you're training it to understand how things work. It's an educational project, if you're interested in experimenting with things like distributed training techniques in JAX, or preference optimisation, this gives you a minimal and hackable library to build on.
在 HN 中查看
wongarsu 2026/04/06 02:54
它也是一个很好的实验基础。如果您有架构改进的想法，您可以花 36 美元在 20 层纳米代码设置上尝试一下，然后再花 200 美元看看它在“全尺寸”纳米代码上的表现如何

Kaparthy 关于改进 nanochat 的笔记 [1] 是我最喜欢阅读的类似博客的内容之一。非常清楚哪些功能有多大影响，以及随着架构的改进，缩放法则如何演变

还有 modded-nanogpt 可以将相同类型的实验转变为训练速跑（并且可能会在途中失去一些严谨性）[2]

1 https://github.com/karpathy/nanochat/blob/master/dev/LOG.md

2 https://github.com/kellerjordan/modded-nanogpt

It's also a great base for experimentation. If you have an idea for an architecture improvement you can try it for $36 on the 20 layer nanocode setting, then for another $200 see how it holds up on the "full scale" nanocode

Kaparthy's notes on improving nanochat [1] are one of my favorite blog-like things to read. Really neat to see which features have how much influence, and how the scaling laws evolve as you improve the architecture

There's also modded-nanogpt which turns the same kind of experimentation into a training speedrun (and maybe loses some rigor on the way) [2]

1 https://github.com/karpathy/nanochat/blob/master/dev/LOG.md

2 https://github.com/kellerjordan/modded-nanogpt

在 HN 中查看
jaboostin 2026/04/06 00:40
作为一个零机器学习经验的人，这是一本超级有趣且易于理解的读物！
As someone with zero ML experience, this was a super interesting and digestible read!
在 HN 中查看
bwfan123 2026/04/06 01:34
同意，很棒的教育工具！为我在编码代理周围绑了一堆东西。
agree, great educational tool ! tied a bunch of things around coding agents for me.
在 HN 中查看
desideratum 2026/04/06 04:21
我非常感谢这些客气话:)
I appreciate the kind words very much : )
在 HN 中查看
vova_hn2 2026/04/06 03:13
> 这是一个向您展示如何端到端训练您自己的克劳德代码的库。

它究竟意味着什么？

Claude Code 是一个所谓的“harness”——为 LLM 构建上下文、调用 LLM、执行工具调用等。它在幕后使用各种 Anthropic 模型。

它也可以使用其他模型 AFAIK。

它无法被“训练”。

抱歉，如果这个评论听起来很挑剔，我只是对术语的不精确使用感到恼火。

> This is a library showing you how to train your own Claude Code end-to-end.

What does it even mean?

Claude Code is a so called "harness" - a thing that builds a context for LLMs, calls LLMs, executes tool calls etc. It uses various Anthropic models under the hood.

It can also use other models AFAIK.

It cannot be "trained".

Sorry if this comment sounds nitpicky, I'm just annoyed by the imprecise use of terminology.

在 HN 中查看
desideratum 2026/04/06 04:21
我明白你的意思，但我不同意。我希望克劳德代码得到一个单独的克劳德基地后训练的支持，该基地已经使用克劳德代码工具和工具集进行了培训。
I see what you mean, but I disagree. I expect that Claude Code is backed by a separate post-train of Claude base which has been trained using the Claude Code harness and toolset.
在 HN 中查看
vova_hn2 2026/04/06 04:29
这当然有可能，但我认为没有理由相信它。
It is possible of course, but I see no reason to believe it.
在 HN 中查看
jasonjmcghee 2026/04/06 06:08
fwiw，与 codex / opencode / pi 等相比，其他模型似乎/据报道在使用 claude 代码时要困难得多。

话虽这么说，还有其他可能的解释

fwiw, other models seem to / are reported to struggle much more with using claude code compared with codex / opencode / pi etc.

that being said, there are other potential explanations

在 HN 中查看
krackers 2026/04/06 03:38
是的，它确实应该是关于工具使用模型的后训练。
Yeah it should really be about post-training a model for tool-use.
在 HN 中查看
redman25 2026/04/06 03:36
不要与 Nanocoder（代理编码工具）混淆。

https://github.com/Nano-Collective/nanocoder

Not to be confused with nanocoder, the agentic coding harness.

https://github.com/Nano-Collective/nanocoder

在 HN 中查看
wg0 2026/04/06 03:49
这真的有效吗？这是 Anthropic 的工作原理吗？

有哪位从业者可以详细说明一下吗？

Does this really work? Does this how Anthropic works?

Any practitioners can elaborate?

在 HN 中查看
desideratum 2026/04/06 04:26
这是该过程的总体简化 - 您通常会使用更多数量级的数据和计算，以及大量的在线强化学习来引发紧急工具使用能力。

许多最近的 OSS 模型都有很棒的技术报告，您可以在其中了解有关此类内容的更多信息： 基米2.5 https://github.com/MoonshotAI/Kimi-K2.5/blob/master/tech_rep... GLM 5 https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.15763 深寻R1 https://arxiv.org/pdf/2501.12948

This is a gross simplification of the process - you would typically use order(s) of magnitude more data and compute, and a substantial amount of online reinforcement learning to elicit emergent tool use capabilities.

Many recent OSS models have great tech reports where you can learn more about these kind of things: Kimi 2.5 https://github.com/MoonshotAI/Kimi-K2.5/blob/master/tech_rep... GLM 5 https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.15763 DeepSeek R1 https://arxiv.org/pdf/2501.12948

在 HN 中查看
